text: Abraham was now old, and the LORD had blessed him in every way.
Abraham told his oldest servant, in charge of everything he owned, to put his hand under his thigh.
He made the servant swear by the LORD not to get a wife for Isaac from the Canaanite women among whom they lived.
Instead he was to go to Abraham's own country and kindred and get a wife for Isaac there.
The servant asked what if the woman would not come back with him — should he take Isaac back to that land?
Abraham told him never to take Isaac back there.
He said the LORD who brought him from his father's house would send his angel ahead to make the journey succeed.
If the woman would not follow him, the servant was released from the oath, but must never take Isaac back.
So the servant put his hand under Abraham's thigh and swore to do this.
The servant took ten of his master's camels loaded with gifts and set out for Aram-naharaim, the city of Nahor.
He made the camels kneel outside the city by a well in the evening, when women came out to draw water.
He prayed for success and for steadfast love to be shown to his master Abraham.
He said he was standing by the spring where the townswomen would come to draw water.
He prayed that the girl who offered water to him and his camels too would be the one appointed for Isaac.
Before he finished speaking, Rebekah, daughter of Bethuel son of Milcah and Nahor, came out with her jar on her shoulder.
She was very beautiful and a virgin; she went down to the spring, filled her jar, and came up.
The servant ran to meet her and asked for a little water from her jar.
She said, drink, my lord, and quickly lowered her jar to give him a drink.
When she finished, she offered to draw water for his camels too, until they had drunk enough.
She emptied her jar into the trough and kept running to the well until she had watered all his camels.
The man watched her silently to see whether the LORD had made his journey succeed.
When the camels finished drinking, he gave her a gold nose ring and two gold bracelets.
He asked whose daughter she was and if there was room in her father's house to lodge.
She answered she was the daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah and Nahor.
She said they had plenty of straw and feed, and room to stay.
The man bowed his head and worshiped the LORD.
He blessed the LORD for leading him straight to his master's relatives, showing steadfast love and faithfulness.
The girl ran and told her mother's household all that had happened.
Rebekah had a brother named Laban, who ran out to the man at the spring.
Seeing the ring and bracelets on his sister and hearing her account, Laban went out to the man standing by the camels.
Laban invited him in, calling him blessed of the LORD, and said he had prepared a place and room for the camels.
The man came into the house; the camels were unloaded and given straw and feed, and water for washing feet.
Food was set before him, but he said he would not eat until he had told his errand.
He said he was Abraham's servant.
He explained the LORD had greatly blessed his master with flocks, herds, silver, gold, servants, and camels.
He said Sarah bore Abraham a son in her old age, and Abraham had given him everything he owned.
His master had made him swear not to take a wife for Isaac from the Canaanite women.
Instead he was to go to Abraham's father's house and family to get a wife for Isaac.
He had asked what if the woman would not follow him.
Abraham had said the LORD would send his angel with him to make the journey succeed.
He would be free of the oath if he went to the family and they refused to give her.
He recounted arriving at the spring that day and praying for success.
He had prayed that the girl who came out to draw water and gave him a drink from her jar would be the one.
And if she also offered to draw for his camels, she would be the one the LORD had appointed for his master's son.
Before he finished praying in his heart, Rebekah came out with her jar, drew water, and he asked her for a drink.
She quickly lowered her jar and offered to water his camels too, and he drank, and she watered the camels.
He asked whose daughter she was, and she said Bethuel, son of Nahor and Milcah, so he put the ring and bracelets on her.
He bowed and worshiped the LORD, who had led him to take his master's brother's granddaughter for his son.
He asked them to say plainly whether they would deal kindly and truly with his master, or not, so he would know which way to turn.
Laban and Bethuel answered that the matter had come from the LORD, and they could say nothing either way.
They told him Rebekah was there before him; let him take her and go, to be the wife of his master's son as the LORD had spoken.
When Abraham's servant heard their words, he bowed to the ground before the LORD.
He brought out silver, gold jewelry, and clothing and gave them to Rebekah, and gave costly gifts to her brother and mother.
He and his men ate and drank and stayed the night, and in the morning he asked to be sent back to his master.
Her brother and mother asked for the girl to stay a while, at least ten days, before she went.
He asked them not to delay him since the LORD had made his journey succeed; let him go to his master.
They said they would call the girl and ask her directly.
They called Rebekah and asked if she would go with this man, and she said she would.
So they sent off Rebekah, her nurse, Abraham's servant, and his men.
They blessed Rebekah, saying may she become the mother of thousands and her offspring possess their enemies' gates.
Rebekah and her young women mounted the camels and followed the man; the servant took Rebekah and departed.
Isaac had just come from Beer-lahai-roi, for he was living in the Negeb.
Isaac went out to walk in the field in the evening, and looking up he saw camels coming.
Rebekah looked up and saw Isaac, and got down from her camel.
She asked the servant who the man walking in the field to meet them was, and he said it was his master, so she covered herself with her veil.
The servant told Isaac everything he had done.
Isaac brought her into his mother Sarah's tent, married Rebekah, and loved her, and was comforted after his mother's death.
